questionBank.question: For series sum_{n=1}^infinity a_n, the nth partial sum S_n is:
questionBank.explanation: By definition, S_n = sum_{k=1}^n a_k (the sum of the first n terms).
questionBank.timeTip: Write first few terms to avoid confusion.

questionBank.question: sum_{n=0}^infinity (1/3)^n converges to:
questionBank.explanation: Geometric series sum = a/(1-r) with a=1, r=1/3, so 1/(1-1/3) = 3/2.

questionBank.question: sum_{n=1}^infinity 1/n^p converges when:
questionBank.explanation: p-series sum 1/n^p converges if and only if p > 1.

questionBank.question: Alternating series test requires:
questionBank.explanation: Need decreasing positive b_n and lim_{n->infinity} b_n = 0.
questionBank.notesSnippet: Check monotonic decrease and limit zero.

questionBank.question: If lim_{n->infinity} |a_{n+1}/a_n| = L < 1, then the series:
questionBank.explanation: Ratio test with L<1 gives absolute convergence.

parts.label: (a)
parts.question: Determine whether the series converges absolutely, conditionally, or diverges.
steps: Therefore original alternating series converges absolutely.
parts.scoring: 1 pt absolute series setup, 1 pt test, 1 pt classification.

parts.label: (b)
parts.question: Is the alternating series error after n terms at most the first omitted term?
parts.finalAnswer: Yes, when terms decrease to 0 in magnitude.
steps: Alternating series error bound applies to decreasing b_n -> 0.

parts.question: Jack is sick and takes a flu medication every hour. He takes 100 mg initially and each dosage drops by 20%. If he keeps taking the medication forever, how much will he have taken? Jill is also sick. Each dosage of hers drops by 30%. If she ends up taking half the amount of medication that Jack eventually took, what was her original dosage?

parts.question: The annual spending by tourists in a resort city is $100 million. 10.1.title: 10.1 Sequences and Partial Sums
10.1.content:
LIM-7 · BC Sequences

Sequences

A sequence {an} is an ordered list. It converges to L if an can be made arbitrarily close to L by taking n large enough.

Series and partial sums

A series Σn=1∞ an is the limit of its partial sums SN = Σn=1N an. If SN → finite limit, the series converges.

nth-term test (divergence)

If limn→∞ an ≠ 0, then Σ an diverges. If the limit is 0, the series may still diverge — this never proves convergence.

AP tip: Name every test you use; show the limit in the nth-term test explicitly.

10.2.title: 10.2 Geometric and p-Series
10.2.content:
LIM-7 · Benchmark series

Geometric series

Σ arn (from n = 0 or 1 — watch the index) converges if |r| < 1 to afirst/(1−r); diverges if |r| ≥ 1

p-series

Σ 1/np converges if p > 1; diverges if 0 < p ≤ 1

Harmonic series Σ 1/n diverges (p = 1). These anchor comparison and limit comparison tests.

Integral test (idea)

If f(x) is positive, continuous, and decreasing for x ≥ 1 and an = f(n), then Σ an and ∫1∞ f(x) dx share convergence behavior.

AP tip: Memorize geometric and p-series conclusions cold; most other series are compared to them.

10.3.title: 10.3–10.5 Alternating, Ratio, Absolute Convergence
10.3.content:
LIM-7 · Convergence tests

Alternating series test

For Σ (−1)n bn with bn > 0: if bn is eventually decreasing and bn → 0, the series converges (often conditionally).

Ratio test

Let L = limn→∞ |an+1/an|. L < 1 ⇒ absolute convergence; L > 1 ⇒ divergence; L = 1 ⇒ inconclusive.

Absolute vs conditional

If Σ|an| converges, Σan converges absolutely. If Σ|an| diverges but Σan converges, convergence is conditional.

AP tip: Alternating series error bound: remainder |RN| ≤ bN+1 under AST hypotheses.
